speaker: interviewer
text: [BACKGROUND NOISE]

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, that's pretty noisy. I guess the door's open. The door's-- [BACKGROUND NOISE] And OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: It's March 15th, 1995. Interview with Sara Hertzberg by Lisa Newman, in Toronto, Canada, in English.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is March 15th, 1995. I'm Lisa Newman, L-I-S-A N-E-W-M-A-N. I'm interviewing Sarah Hertzberg, S-A-R-A H-E-R-T-Z-B-E-R-G, in Toronto, Canada, in English.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, Lisa.

speaker: interviewee
text: It's a lot of work involved.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you are?

speaker: interviewee
text: I am Sara Hertzberg. And I was born in Poland, in 1925, in a city of Wegrow, a province of Lublin. And my family-- oh, no. My father's name was [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] no, Yosef Szejnberg, and my mother's name was Shifra Szejnberg. And I had three-- two sisters except me, and three brothers. And the oldest one Velvel. The second one was Freidke. The third one was Chavche. And then was Usher, Feivel, Chiel, and me, Sara.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were there youngest of seven.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was the youngest, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: You told me your father was a [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, we come from-- he was from [NON-ENGLISH] rabbi's [NON-ENGLISH]. And in our house was a shtiebel that we had a minyan or more people, just people, I suppose they were from the [NON-ENGLISH] rabbi's crowd. And every Saturday there was a special davenen in our house, was a davenen. And when it comes Yom Tov, also they all gathered in our kitchen. That house was about five rooms. And the first one was a kitchen and then it was beside-- was a room. And the bedroom was divided.

speaker: interviewer
text: So in that first room where the kitchen was-- it was like a living room to us, maybe the dining room-- there was [NON-ENGLISH] and a [NON-ENGLISH] And there was the men were davening.

speaker: interviewee
text: But there was a little partition. And in the side where the bedroom was, the woman used to come to daven. And they mainly come when it was high holidays. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And that was going on for quite a few years, generation by generation, when I wasn't born yet. I even remember my grandmother was living not far in a little city, not far from us.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your father would lead the services.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, but the choir was my brothers. And the girls, the sisters, we used to help to sing, from behind. So that was, that was, you know, the choir I was. And my oldest brother was very educated too. He used to go and help out my father to daven. And Chiel was special because he went to the yeshiva. And he had [NON-ENGLISH] and cantor. [NON-ENGLISH]-- he was-- like he got his degree for Rabbi.

speaker: interviewer
text: Like semikhah.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, well, we call it [NON-ENGLISH]. I don't know what you call it in here. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was the yeshiva? Do you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was going in [NON-ENGLISH] to the yeshiva, because my uncles were living there. And they gave him like-- in the yeshiva in Poland, they used to go every day eat in a different-- to a different family. It was Thursday here, Wednesday there, you know, like they used to. And I was going to public school then. And in 19-- well, it was a big anti-Semitism in our city, too. There was a day when the-- they take people to military, you know, the--

speaker: interviewer
text: To the army.

speaker: interviewee
text: To the army. Then they could do what they want. So all the Jewish people were hiding in their houses and closing up all the windows and all the doors, and special girls. Because they used to drink and they use to-- they use to wear the berets with the thing. Now what I can't stand if I see somebody in here with that, it reminds me of them. And they came in, and they turned around the whole city. They were allowed that day to do whatever they want. So everybody was hiding. When it comes before Pesach, we were also in very big trouble. Because they accused that the Jewish people kill-- they got to have blood to have their matzos. We used to bake matzos because they were so kosher, my father, that we used to bake matzos in our own place for a lot of the Hasidim.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I was-- you know, I looked at it. I was rolling them, and there was girls matched up with [NON-ENGLISH] because there was boys and girls working that were baking matzos for weeks before Pesach. And-- so it was a rough time for us, when it came Pesach. They usually made a pogrom, or they did something bad. So one year I remember when we were sitting at the seder-- and usually my father start at 12:00 at night. And then we had a sleep before. Put on the kettle with the kitchens, and the kids around, and we had to sit and listen everything.

speaker: interviewee
text: But some guys came in from, from the city, from outside the holy grounds, you know. And they throw in a dead pig to the house. And good enough, they didn't start hitting us or killing us, but it was a big joke. They start laughing, and they just walked out. After that incident, was a lot, a lot of things. When it was a wedding in the city, a Polish wedding, it was a terrible thing. Because they always told us they going to kill my father, and they're going to kill everybody, whoever a Jew comes along. Because the wedding was in private houses. And when they start dancing and one guy take away the bride just to dance, the other one knifed them. And in the morning, you heard somebody was killed. They were living like that. But specially Jews were very afraid.

speaker: interviewer
text: So my father went, and we were very scared to live. Even we were very good with the police, but they were also goyim. They were also on their side, they weren't on our side. So my father went, and he got a paper that he's allowed to buy a revolver. And he did. He had the revolver. And-- so when it cames to a wedding, we were very afraid. Because they always say tonight you're going to be all killed.

speaker: interviewee
text: The houses were built just from wood. It was very little protection. The doors were only you know, with a thing like that. And that was a protection. I used to be the-- the scary at night. When everybody went to sleep, I went down and went in the back and in the front. And I was locking all the doors. And we had cows. We have land, like every-- like a farm. And we did all kind of things to make a living. And so when my father got that revolver and we knew another wedding is coming up, he went out and he bought a big bottle of-- we call it vitriol. It was like a poison some kind. And we locked him up in a cupboard. We, we hide him behind the clothes. And he sit there with his gun. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And my mother and all of us, the kids, we were still small, we weren't grown up yet. They put in bottles that vitriol. And they told us if somebody forced the door opens up, the first thing, just give it to him in the eyes, so he should get blind. That was our-- this was our guns, you know. And we were shivering, because that was terrible. And later was impossible even to do the seders. So-- because they knocked all the windows out. And my father used to go out and shoot in the sky two, three times. But they still weren't afraid.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we figure out that they made-- I don't know what you call it in English. For the windows from steel, that at night you can close that. So when they throw the stones, they would move away, and it wouldn't break the windows out.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we lived in that condition, scary. Like, I went to public school, and I never had my name Sara. My name was [NON-ENGLISH]. And the most of my friends, English kids-- Polish kids, they didn't know my real name. My name was [NON-ENGLISH], and I'm really Sara. Sara I became when I came to Canada. I took my own name.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that was-- what it-- we had a president, and his name was-- I told you. [? Pidulski? ?]

speaker: interviewee
text: Huh?

speaker: interviewer
text: [? Pidulski? ?]

speaker: interviewee
text: Pilsudski.

speaker: interviewer
text: Pilsudski. I'm sorry.

speaker: interviewee
text: Pilsudski. And he was very, very good to the Jewish people. We prayed for him. We called him-- he's our grandfather, father. Because then, when he was alive, nothing happened. Even all these incidents happened, and it's private. The police-- we couldn't do a issue of it. Oh, and there's another thing. When I was a teenager, we started going out in the city, like you want to go out with boys or girls. And I had a group of girlfriends, what I have them here. I have the picture of the girlfriends in here. And so we were afraid to go in the nicer streets. Like you would say here on University Avenue or someplace, you know, more fancy street, Young Street. We were only cruising around one street, back and forth, like where the Jewish people used to live. Because if you go there, the guys could take away the girls and did anything whatever they want and beat up the boys. And you never came out alive.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you yourself as a child ever attacked or threatened?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No, I-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] from mine place to the school was quite to go, far away. And it wasn't like we have here sidewalks. It was you have to jump through water, the water was everywhere. It was you know, like European style. And when I was going to school, it was in the summer. And three hooligans came across, like you see in [NON-ENGLISH] with the-- And they start joking, asking me, what's your name and so on. And they made dirty jokes at me. But I was very scared. But I escaped. And I went to school, and that was going-- was-- was quite a walk from one place. And it was a lot of this Polish and Jewish were too. But wasn't so organized. We, we just mixed. We didn't tell who we are or something like that, you know. We-- I belonged to Hashomer Hatzair. when I grow up later, but everything was in between the Jewish quarters.

speaker: interviewer
text: And in 1939, it was the last year of my school. And we went for a trip to see Warsaw. I guess we never was any place. My father treated me, I think it was 20 zlotys to go. And we went away for a few days, to see where Pilsudski used to live. Can I show the picture then? We'll show it at the end.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And we came and we saw all the-- he lived in a belvedere, it's just like the White House. And there was his spoon and his bed and everything where he was living. And when I came back, I applied-- I want to go to the university. And we didn't know what's gonna happen. I don't remember who took over, because Hitler came in to [INAUDIBLE] already. And they wouldn't let Jewish kids go to university. They wouldn't take us, for no reason. But it's just like that. And then my father died. But-- I-- he died-- no, he died even before, in 1938. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: What did he die of?

speaker: interviewee
text: Hmm?

speaker: interviewer
text: What did he die of?

speaker: interviewee
text: He died-- he was going from one city to the other buying with my brother, buying cows and all kind of-- chickens, all kind. And they took it to the, to the big city. And then, then he went. They bought milk-- and we had a place we used to make sour cream from it, cream. Then they took it to the city. But when he came to the city, all the poor Jewish people knew Yosef is coming, so they stand in a big line. And he-- the first thing, he give to them, all of them. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I want to go very much university. But it's no way that--

speaker: interviewer
text: I just want to take you back. He died of?

speaker: interviewee
text: Huh?

speaker: interviewer
text: Your father, he died from what?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father died because when he was going with that wagon-- so from the bushes came out. They used to come out and rob them and all kind. And we were-- every night we were crying whenever he went, he didn't come back, it was a terrible tragedy in the house. So he didn't come yet, probably they kill him or something like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they turned around the wagon with him. And he fell with his-- on a big stone. And he ruined his kidneys. And he started getting sick with the kidneys. So we-- we had every day a doctor, and we couldn't do very much for him, because there was no help. The doctor came in, gave him something. And, and he died, because the heart gave up too. And he died.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then I couldn't be home without him. It was so terrible. My mother used to cry and everything. And I said, I want to go any place, I want to do anything. And I was the youngest in the family, and--

speaker: interviewer
text: So my mother had two brothers in Bialystok. And we write to them. And they said I could come, one-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I could come there, and I'll be with them. And, and I'll go to school there. So I had a auntie [NON-ENGLISH], and I had some family-- That was your father's sister.

speaker: interviewee
text: My father's sister.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Brajndel. And she had two children, Rivka and Moshe. And her husband's name I don't remember. What is the picture in here.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I went there, just as a child. I went there because I wanted-- I want to know them. Just like somebody would say that I never, never see them again or something.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I went there. And I stayed with them about a week. And then I came back. And I went to-- to see mine other uncle, what I have on the picture here, with-- with his family. And he was-- oh, I forgot their name. The-- the other one was in [NON-ENGLISH], and this was in another city in Poland. Now I've forgotten of the name.

speaker: interviewer
text: So these were visits.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. When I came back, and then I went to Bialystok.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you would have been how old?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, when I finished school, I was 14 years old. Because it's seven grades, and I start when I was seven. And that was in 1939. And I took whatever I could. And that apron I was wearing, my brother Usher, he had some money. He says he's gonna buy me-- maybe they accept me, maybe not. But he bought me that, and that's where I have the picture.

speaker: interviewer
text: And we'll see that in the picture.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I came to Bialystok, and I met my uncles.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what were their name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Their names was-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] let's see, if I knew it before, I would have tell you.

speaker: interviewer
text: It'll come back.

speaker: interviewee
text: Greenberg or Steinberg, something like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you stayed actually with--

speaker: interviewee
text: I stayed there, and I wanna register for school. In the meantime, the war broke out. So I was left in Bialystok.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when the Hitler came in, and it was terrible. He just start going to-- he took out the rabbi from our city the first thing. And he had a lot of daughters with the family. And they told him to clean the street. This is in Wegrow you mean?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was in Bialystok.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh. In Bialystok.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And they kill him. As he bend down, they knifed him. And that was the first thing. And later it was terrible.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you saw that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And later, was going on, like the German [INAUDIBLE] was terrible. They were grabbing young people to work and all kind of things. But it wasn't they took us to concentrations. It just started the whole thing. But they bombed. They bombed the whole city. Everything fall apart. And people were hiding and running, they didn't know where to go. They didn't know what to do with themselves.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you able to be in touch with your family at this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no, no. Because they were-- they were there, and I was here. And it was no letters, no writing. The bombs were falling. Who-- who knew where, what? Nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I was living with my uncle Sruel, Sruel-- Parkman, Parkman. Their name was Parkman, because my mother's name was Parkman. And then-- it took a while. We were with them, we were scared. We were here, we were there, we were hiding. And it took a long time. Young kids used to run through the brook, swim, and run away. Because they said this city and this city, they went to on Russian side.

speaker: interviewee
text: But later, the German made an agreement with the Russian that they take a half, and the other half will be Russia. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So it happens to be what I was-- it was the Russian took us. But they were just very close, and we were going to visit our, our other relatives in the German side too. And there was left one cousin-- what I told you, I have her here in the picture too. And she got left with two kids. And her husband, they took him away. He went to the army, or they kill him. I don't remember what happened to him.

speaker: interviewer
text: And her name was?

speaker: interviewee
text: Her name was also Freidke. But the second name I don't remember. She's got another sister, Gittel, what I have in here the picture, with her husband. And I used to go and visit her all the time, and see how it is. And like later, I start making sandwiches, because there's a lot of people were coming from Hitler's side. They were struggling, and they were coming to dying and typhus and all kind of sickness. And I went and made sandwiches to-- for us and for them, whatever had money. So I went out. I bought salami in a factory, in a Jewish kosher place, and bread. And we start to help each other, you know, because people were dying in the streets and so on. And-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I don't remember exactly what happened later. Because I know later on, the war broke out. And Hitler, without giving a sign, he just-- the Russian were so good with them. They bake bread, and they send wagons of bread to the German. And they started a war so suddenly that nobody expected it. And you know, and they-- so they start to come in in the other, in the other side too. But still, the Russian were pushing them back. It was going on a war between-- they didn't get it the whole thing right away. It took a long time. And the meantime, I had no place to go, because the Russian, again, when they were on this side, they took all the people what they own-- they own business and things like that. And they send them away, to Siberia. But in Russia, you're not allowed to have any business on your own. Everything belongs to the government, and you work for them. And they, whatever they give you, this is-- is enough.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I couldn't be with my uncles. So I came back, and I stayed with this young couple, with my cousins. And they took him away in the war, and they took the other cousin's husband away. So I was with them. Then I found a girlfriend. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Her name was Baltscha. And they were also about seven kids. The mother died, and her husband-- her father left them, and he went to live with another woman or something.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they accepted me to their house. And one day, the Russian decide to see if we are communists or we are, we are-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] what do you call it when you betray the country--

speaker: interviewer
text: Traitors?

speaker: interviewee
text: Spy. Spies. They didn't trust us. Because people were doing business, going still to Germany, and still go to buy in Russia and bring into Germany. They risk their lives, because they want to see their families. The meantime, they bought gold, and, you know, it was going on something like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: So one day, they let out papers in the streets that they want all this [NON-ENGLISH]-- they called us [NON-ENGLISH]-- where we came-- to register, if we want to go back to see our family. We didn't know that they taking already people in the concentrations. We didn't know what's doing in the other side at all. So everybody had to register where he lives and give the address and everything. And I said like that-- if I have to die, I want to die with my family. What, I'm alone, I'm 14 years old, what am I gonna do here in that life? What's doing in this side, it's terrible too. And I left them all-- I want to go back. Anyways, I registered.

speaker: interviewee
text: But when I came to Bialystok, I start to singing in a big choir. It was a big conductor, Sneerson from Warsaw. He was a very famous guy. We organized for boys and girls. And we used to sing in the streets for the Russians on the-- all over, we went to perform. And I met that girl Baltscha. And one night, we had the performance. And it was I think on a Friday. Friday night she says to me, I don't want you to go home by yourself. It's late after the concert. Come sleep with us. That night the Russian came with police and with, with, with dogs and with, with everything. And wherever they had the address, as they pulled them all out, put all these people in-- in trains. And trains where you-- where you-- when you buy cows, horses-- in trains like that, without nothing, just plain dirty. And they threw 'em all in there in. They gave them a lot of herring. But they didn't give 'em water to drink. I shouldn't-- I always obey to say that. Because if wouldn't be for the Russian, a lot of people wouldn't be alive. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But there was plenty going on in behind in Russia, too. I mean, Stalin did a lot of dirty tricks to the people. But then the other hand, the Russian people were very nice to us. Wherever we came, they took us in, they give us food, and they let us stay. Of course, I always find Jewish families, and one help the other. And I mean, what was going on in Germany is not a compare. On the other side, I mean, what it was divided in Poland. I don't call it Germany, it was still-- you know, but they took it over.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I can't say that. Because if I say, maybe the government, I didn't know what was doing, what they have in behind. But they did a dirty joke. They took everybody out, whoever it was.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I was a survivor. I survived because I was sleeping not at home. They couldn't find me. And they took them all to Siberia to [NON-ENGLISH] to-- the end, there where-- where there was no-- no life. There is just snow, and there is just absolutely nothing. And they told them, [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]. If you don't get used to it, you going to die like a dog.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you learn about this?

speaker: interviewee
text: People came back. They were alive, and not everybody died. And a lot of people died. Like my husband was taken in plan too. He was in the Polish army. And then he was all through this. And then they took him-- also on this same one day-- and they took him also away.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they sent him away to [NON-ENGLISH]. And his name is Leo Hertzberg I just want to mention that. And over there, he was lucky because he was a tailor. So he went into this family, the officer of the whole thing, from that-- it's like a camp, I call it. Families, when they came with fathers and mothers and they, they survive. They-- they sew, they did all kind. But single people, it was terrible.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he went. He was a cook for that big officer there, from the-- this place. And he was there with them. And one day, he tried to run away.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where is there to run? It's no [INAUDIBLE], but he tried. So they let dogs, and the dogs grabbed him all over. They almost eat him up with the teeth. And he had to come back.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he was there till I think-- I don't remember which year, 19-- maybe '42 or something. They let him out.

speaker: interviewer
text: We're gonna hear-- we're gonna hear about him later. But just to come back to your story--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: You're now 14?

speaker: interviewee
text: I am, I am in Bialystok, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: You're how old?

speaker: interviewee
text: I am 14.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I am in Bialystok. I'm sleeping with my girlfriend-- [LAUGHS] with my girlfriend's house.

speaker: interviewer
text: And all your family have gone.

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know anything about them yet.

speaker: interviewer
text: There's no one left there.

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I don't know anything. They are on the other side, and I'm sure that they're all alive.

speaker: interviewer
text: I see.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they don't live through what we will live through because--

speaker: interviewer
text: And your uncle's been taken away?

speaker: interviewee
text: The uncle's been taken away.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your cousin's been taken away.

speaker: interviewee
text: Finished. We don't know anything about them. The Russian took them away.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when I was hiding, when I was in my girlfriend's house, sleeping over, a few days later, I want to go and see what happened to mine cousin, if she's alive. Because with two kids, there's no food, and it's terrible. And it's a very bad situation. And-- so I pick myself up, and I-- I went.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I went with side streets and things like that. And I was afraid, because where mine cousin used to live, there was a guy. And he was watching for those people. If he see somebody, right away he reported them. In Russian it was called [RUSSIAN]. He was taking care on that section of the people. And so when I came up to my cousin, it was on a Friday. And I was happy that she's alive and the kids, and we start talking. And I'm-- and she was living in a small house, on the third floor. And I'm looking through the window, and I see there is two-- two Russian [NON-ENGLISH] over there-- it's like here the MP-- and this guy with them. They coming right up the steps for me. And I had washed my head. I had very shaggy clothes. I had just a little skirt what I made from a shirt what I took from the house and a little top. And my shoes were just a pair of sandals what I made myself. And when I saw them coming, I knew they gonna take me, and I'll go where all the other went. And they-- it's the end of it. So I jumped down from the third floor. I opened up a window, and I jumped down through the back. And I fell in a backyard. And then I start running through the fence, from one place to the other and streets and streets, until I came back to my girlfriend.

speaker: interviewer
text: We're going to stop just for a minute and take a short break.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. I could talk still, if you want. I'm not tired.
